title: Lateral retractor system for minimizing muscle damage in spinal surgery
abstract: A lateral retractor system for forming a surgical pathway to a patient's intervertebral disc space includes a single dilator and a retractable dual-tapered-blade assembly. The single dilator may feature a narrow rectangular body for insertion at an insertion orientation parallel to the fibers of the patient's psoas muscle and at an approximate 45-degree angle to the patient's spine. The retractable dual-tapered-blade assembly consists of only two blade subassemblies, each having a blade bordered by adjustable wings, along with built-in lighting and video capabilities. The dual-tapered-blade assembly may be passed over the single dilator at the insertion orientation and rotated approximately 45-50 degrees from the insertion orientation to a final rotated orientation parallel to the intervertebral disc space before the two blade subassemblies are retracted away from one another to create the surgical pathway. Other embodiments are also disclosed.
claims:
Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A dilator for use with a lateral retraction assembly for forming a surgical pathway to a patientâs intervertebral disc space, the dilator having a narrow body defined by opposing first and second flat surfaces that extend proximally-to-distally from a proximal end to a distal end, wherein the first and the second flat surfaces adjacent to the distal end of the body taper to meet at a leading distal edge along a first tapered surface and an opposing second tapered surface, the leading distal edge of the dilator configured for insertion at an insertion orientation in which the first and the second flat surfaces are disposed at approximately a 45-degree angle to the patientâs intervertebral disc space and parallel with a plurality of psoas muscle fibers of the patient. 2. The dilator of claim 1 , wherein an entirety of each of the first and the second tapered surfaces of the dilator form an active neuromonitoring tip configured to continuously and simultaneously detect an impingement upon one or more nerve structures located adjacent to any portion of a circumference of the active neuromonitoring tip. 3. The dilator of claim 2, wherein the active neuromonitoring tip comprises a set of horizontal neurosensing wires embedded within the first and the second tapered surfaces, the set of the horizontal neurosensing wires in electronic communication with at least one longitudinal conducting wire extending from the neuromonitoring tip to the proximal end of the dilator. 4. The dilator of claim 1 , wherein the leading distal edge of the dilator is configured for separating and traversing the plurality of the psoas muscle fibers and cutting through one or more layers of fascia of the patient. 5. The dilator of claim 1 , wherein the first and the second flat surfaces taper to meet along first and second longitudinal edges extending between the 

 proximal and the distal ends. 6. The dilator of claim 5, wherein the first and the second longitudinal edges are configured for separating and traversing the plurality of the psoas muscle fibers and cutting through one or more layers of fascia of the patient. 7. The dilator of claim 1 , the leading distal edge of the dilator further configured for rotation about a longitudinal center axis of the dilator from the insertion orientation in which the first and the second flat surfaces are disposed at approximately the 45-degree angle to the patientâs intervertebral disc space and parallel with the plurality of the psoas muscle fibers of the patient to a rotated orientation in which the first and the second flat surfaces are disposed parallel to the patientâs intervertebral disc space. 8. A dilation system for minimizing damage to a patientâs psoas muscle fibers when forming a surgical pathway to an intervertebral disc space of a patientâs spine, the dilation system comprising: a dilator, the dilator comprising: a rectangular body having opposing planar surfaces extending between a proximal end and a distal end along opposing longitudinal sides; opposing longitudinal extensions protruding outward from each of the opposing longitudinal sides of the rectangular body to terminate at opposing longitudinal edges; and a distal extension protruding outward from the distal end of the rectangular body to terminate at a leading distal edge, the leading distal edge configured for insertion at an insertion orientation in which the leading distal edge is disposed at approximately a 45-degree angle to the intervertebral disc space of the patientâs spine and parallel with the patientâs psoas muscle fibers. 9. The dilation system of claim 8, the dilator further comprising an active neuromonitor distributed evenly over an entirety of an outer surface of the distal 5UB5TITUTE SHEET (RULE 26) 

 extension, the active neuromonitor configured to continuously and simultaneously detect an impingement upon one or more nerve structures located adjacent to any portion of the distal extension without rotation of the dilator about a longitudinal axis. 10. The dilation system of claim 9, wherein the active neuromonitor comprises a set of neurosensing wires horizontally traversing a width of the distal extension, the set of the neurosensing wires in electronic communication with at least one longitudinal conducting wire extending from the distal extension to the proximal end of the dilator. 1 1 . The dilation system of claim 8, wherein the leading distal edge is configured to cut through a layer of fascia of the patient and traverse the patientâs psoas muscle fibers at the insertion orientation. 12. The dilation system of claim 1 1 , further comprising a k-wire extending longitudinally through a center of the dilator for securing the dilator within the intervertebral disc space at the insertion orientation in which the leading distal edge is disposed at approximately the 45-degree angle to the intervertebral disc space of the patientâs spine and parallel with the patientâs psoas muscle fibers, wherein the dilator is further configured for rotation about the k-wire from the insertion orientation to a rotated orientation in which the leading distal edge of the dilator is disposed parallel to the intervertebral disc space of the patientâs spine. 13. The dilation system of claim 8, further comprising a dual-blade retractor assembly including two opposing retractable blades, each of the opposing retractable blades having a planar inner surface and an outer surface, the dual-blade retractor assembly configured to pass over the dilator at the insertion orientation such that the planar inner surfaces of the opposing retractable blades contact the opposing planar surfaces of the dilator. 14. The dilation system of claim 8, wherein the dilator is formed of a sterilizable metal or a sterilizable plastic. 15. A method of forming a surgical pathway to an intervertebral disc space of a patient, comprising: providing a dilator, the dilator including: a narrow body defined by two opposing planar surfaces that extend proximally-to-distally from a proximal end to a distal end; and a distal protrusion extending distally from the distal end of the narrow body, the distal protrusion formed by two opposing tapered surfaces that taper inward toward a center of the narrow body to meet and terminate at a leading distal edge; positioning the dilator over the intervertebral disc space at an insertion orientation in which the leading distal edge is disposed parallel to a psoas muscle of the patient and at approximately a 45-degree angle to the intervertebral disc space; using the leading distal edge of the dilator, traversing a plurality of fibers of the psoas muscle of the patient until the leading distal edge of the dilator spans the intervertebral disc space at the insertion orientation; passing a k-wire through a longitudinal center of the dilator; and securing the dilator within the intervertebral disc space at the insertion orientation. 16. The method of claim 15, further comprising: prior to the traversing the plurality of the fibers of the psoas muscle of the patient, cutting through a layer of intervening fascia of the patient using the leading distal edge of the dilator. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein the dilator further includes two opposing longitudinal protrusions, each of the opposing longitudinal protrusions extending outward from an opposing longitudinal side of the narrow body between 

 the proximal end and the distal end, and each of the opposing longitudinal protrusions formed by two opposing tapered surfaces that taper inward toward a center of the narrow body to meet and terminate at a longitudinal edge. 18. The method of claim 17, further comprising rotating the dilator about the k-wire from the insertion orientation in which the leading distal edge is disposed parallel to the psoas muscle of the patient and at approximately the 45-degree angle to the intervertebral disc space to a rotated orientation in which the leading distal edge is parallel to the intervertebral disc space, such that the plurality of the psoas muscle fibers are separated by the opposing longitudinal protrusions of the dilator. 19. The method of claim 18, wherein the rotating comprises rotating the dilator through 45-50 degrees about the k-wire. 20. The method of claim 18, further comprising: during the positioning the dilator, the cutting through the layer of the intervening fascia of the patient, the traversing the plurality of the fibers of the psoas muscle, the passing the k-wire through the longitudinal center of the dilator, the securing the dilator within the intervertebral disc space at the insertion orientation, and the rotating the dilator about the k-wire from the insertion orientation to the rotated orientation, using an active neuromonitoring tip distributed over an entirety of the distal protrusion to continuously and simultaneously monitor for an encroachment upon one or more nerve structures of the patient located adjacent to any portion of an outer circumference of the distal protrusion.
